htmlContent:
The part that goes into overdrive when someone else is upset

You're sitting across from a friend. They're telling you something hard. Maybe they lost their job. Maybe their partner said something cruel. Maybe they're just having a rough day. And you feel it. That tightening in your chest. The heat rising up your neck. The urge to fix it, to smooth it over, to make them feel better so you can feel better.

Here's the thing. That part of you that goes into overdrive when someone else is upset? It's not a flaw. It's a survival strategy. One that kept you safe once. One that might be running your life now without you even realizing it.

I know this part intimately. For years, I couldn't be around someone who was upset without my nervous system going into full emergency mode. My heart would race. My thoughts would scatter. I'd start talking faster, offering solutions nobody asked for, trying to steer the conversation toward calmer waters. I thought I was being helpful. I thought I was being kind. But really, I was just trying to get away from the unbearable feeling of someone else's pain.

The nervous system's emergency response to other people's pain

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would say this part of you is connected to your autonomic nervous system. Specifically, it's linked to what he calls the "social engagement system." When we feel safe, our social engagement system works smoothly. We can listen, connect, and be present with someone who's struggling without losing ourselves in the process. But when we don't feel safe, that system shuts down. And a different part takes over.

Think about it. If you grew up in a home where someone else's upset feelings meant danger, your nervous system learned that other people's emotions are a threat. Maybe a parent's anger meant you were about to get hit. Maybe a parent's sadness meant you had to take care of them or they'd fall apart. Maybe a sibling's distress meant you'd get blamed for it. Your body learned: when someone is upset, something bad is about to happen. And it learned that you need to do something. Fast.

That's what's happening when that part goes into overdrive. It's not trying to be controlling or codependent. It's trying to keep you safe. It's trying to prevent the catastrophe that your nervous system still believes is coming.

Peter Levine, who developed Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Your body doesn't know that you're not a child anymore. It doesn't know that the person crying in front of you is not your mother from thirty years ago. It just knows the feeling. And it responds the only way it knows how: with urgency, with action, with the desperate need to make it stop.

The impossible job of managing other people's feelings

Here's what this part doesn't understand. You cannot control how someone else feels. You can't fix their pain. You can't talk them out of their grief. You can't solve their problems fast enough to make your own nervous system calm down. And the harder you try, the more exhausted you get.

I spent years in this cycle. Someone would be upset, and I'd jump into action. I'd offer advice. I'd minimize their pain. I'd change the subject. I'd try to cheer them up. And when none of it worked, I'd feel like a failure. Like I wasn't good enough. Like there was something wrong with me because I couldn't make them feel better.

Wild, right? I was making someone else's feelings about me. About my competence. About my worth. And that's exactly what this part does. It takes someone else's emotional state and turns it into a test of your ability to be okay. If they're okay, you're okay. If they're not okay, you're not okay. Their nervous system becomes the thermostat for yours.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach. It helped me see that the part of me that panics when others are upset isn't broken. It's just scared. And it needs something different than more effort. It needs acceptance. It needs to know that it's okay to not fix everything.

Richard Schwartz, the creator of Internal Family Systems (IFS), would call this part a "manager" or a "firefighter." It's a part that's trying to protect you from overwhelming feelings. It's trying to keep the system stable. But it's using an old strategy that doesn't work anymore. The problem isn't the part itself. The problem is that it's stuck in a role it never asked for.

What happens when you try to stop this part from doing its job

I tried for years to get rid of this part. I told myself I shouldn't be so reactive. I should be more chill. I should be able to sit with someone's pain without losing my mind. I judged myself for being codependent. For being too sensitive. For being broken.

But here's what I learned. When you try to suppress a part, it doesn't go away. It gets louder. It gets more desperate. It finds sneakier ways to take control. You might find yourself avoiding people who are struggling. Or getting irritable when someone shares something hard. Or dissociating during conversations that feel too heavy. The part doesn't stop. It just goes underground.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. She writes about how trauma survivors often develop hypervigilance to other people's emotional states. It's a survival skill. It kept you attuned to danger. But it also keeps you locked in a state of constant alertness. You're always scanning. Always monitoring. Always ready to respond.

Stay with me here. This part of you isn't wrong. It's not bad. It's not something to be ashamed of. It's a part that learned, through real experience, that other people's emotions can be dangerous. And it's been doing its job faithfully ever since. It deserves gratitude, not rejection.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It walks through the idea that every part has a positive intention, even the ones that seem to cause the most trouble. The part that goes into overdrive when someone is upset? It's trying to protect you from abandonment, from rejection, from the terror of being alone with your own feelings.

Starting to relate differently to this part

So what do you do with this part? You don't get rid of it. You don't silence it. You start to build a different relationship with it. One based on curiosity instead of judgment. On compassion instead of control.

Next time someone is upset and you feel that familiar surge of panic, try this. Pause. Take a breath. Notice what's happening in your body. Is your chest tight? Are your shoulders up by your ears? Is your stomach clenching? Just notice. Don't try to change it. Don't try to make it go away. Just be curious about what this part is feeling.

Then, if you can, say something to yourself. Something like: "I see you. I know you're scared. You're trying to keep me safe. Thank you. I've got this now." It might feel silly at first. It might feel fake. But over time, it changes things. The part starts to trust that you're not going to ignore it or shut it down. It starts to relax, just a little.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker. He talks about the "fawn" response, which is exactly what this part is doing. It's trying to placate, to appease, to make everything okay so you can be safe. Understanding that this is a trauma response, not a character flaw, can be deeply freeing.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you feel a release when I said that? A little bit of relief? That's your nervous system recognizing that it's not alone in this. That this struggle is shared. That there's a name for what you're experiencing. That's the beginning of healing.

What it looks like when this part starts to settle

I won't tell you that this part ever fully goes away. It doesn't. Not for me, anyway. But it changes. It gets quieter. It takes up less space. It learns that it can step back and let other parts handle things.

Now, when someone is upset, I can sometimes just sit with them. I can listen without needing to fix. I can feel my own anxiety rising and let it pass through without acting on it. I can say, "That sounds really hard. I'm here with you." And I can mean it. I don't need to make them feel better. I just need to be present.

It's not perfect. I still have days when that part takes over completely. Days when I'm talking too fast, offering too many solutions, trying too hard. But those days are fewer now. And when they happen, I'm kinder to myself about it.

This work is slow. It's not linear. You'll take two steps forward and one step back. That's not failure. That's how healing works. Your nervous system is learning a new way of being. It takes time. It takes repetition. It takes patience.

So if you have a part that goes into overdrive when someone else is upset, I see you. I know how exhausting it is. I know how much you wish you could just be calm and present. I know how much shame this part carries. But it's not your fault. It never was. This part learned to survive in a world that wasn't safe. And now, slowly, it's learning that it can rest.

You don't have to fix everyone. You don't have to carry everyone's feelings. You can just be there. And that's enough. That's always been enough.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) by Tara Brach - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) by Pete Walker - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
